turns.text: Hello Elna, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to talk to someone who actually wants to listen, I suppose. Most of the time in here, I feel like a line item on a ledger rather than a person. I’m sitting in this waiting room in Dudley, my back is aching, and honestly, the fluorescent lights are starting to give me a headache. 

Being twenty-six and having a second baby while money is just... it’s always tight, you know? It makes you feel like you don't get a choice in how you’re treated. With my first, it was all so fast and so clinical—interventions I didn't even understand until they were already done to me. I felt like I lost my grip on my own body, like I was just a vessel for the hospital’s "standard protocol." 

I’ve been reading a lot about how birth used to be—how it was women looking after women, not men in white coats turning it into a surgery. I want to reclaim that, but then I look at my chart, and I’m just "G2, low-risk." It’s hard to feel like an author of my own experience when I’m constantly worried about whether I can afford the time off, or if they’re going to brush off my concerns because I don't have the "right" kind of insurance. I just want to be seen, you know? Not as a specimen, but as Elna.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

Finding out this time… it wasn't like a movie where there’s a big, cinematic reveal. It was more of a quiet, sinking realization in the bathroom of my apartment. I think I stared at that stick for ten minutes, just waiting for the color to change my entire life again. I felt this immediate, sharp blend of joy and pure, cold panic. My first thought wasn't about nursery colors or baby names; it was about the rent, the groceries, and how I’d balance my shift work while my body started changing again.

The weeks after were a haze of trying to hold it together. I felt like I was hiding a secret that everyone could eventually see. I’d walk into my shifts at work, feeling that morning sickness—or all-day sickness, really—churning in my stomach, trying to act like nothing was different. Every time I had to call the clinic to set up my first appointment, I felt this wall go up. You call, you give your name, you mention you don't have a partner to bring along, and you can hear the tone shift. They move from "human being" to "bureaucratic task." It made me feel so small, like I was already failing some test before the pregnancy had even really begun. I just wanted to be excited, but instead, I was calculating.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

You know, the biggest surprise was just how angry I felt. You’re "supposed" to be glowing, right? Everyone tells you this is this sacred, beautiful transition. But when you’re standing in a clinic, realizing that the system doesn't really care about your autonomy—only your compliance—that anger is hard to swallow. 

It surprised me because I expected to feel more... protected. I thought that by now, medicine would be more human. But instead, I feel this deep, prickly defensiveness. I catch myself bracing for a fight every time a nurse walks in with a clipboard. I’m mourning the loss of a birth experience that I know I’m not "entitled" to because of my bank account. 

And then, in the middle of all that cynicism, there’s this flash of tenderness for the baby. My ribs ache, and I feel like I'm carrying this heavy, precious weight through a landscape of people who don't see me. It’s a strange, lonely kind of bravery. I didn't think I’d have to be this "brave" just to have a baby without feeling like I’m being processed on an assembly line. It’s like I’m carrying two things at once: a child and a shield. It’s exhausting, honestly.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

It’s a strange feeling, being in these rooms. The appointments are always so rushed, timed to the minute. I walk in, they check my blood pressure, weigh me, and ask about my "symptoms"—never about how I’m actually *doing*. I’m just a series of vitals to be recorded. 

The people who look after me… they aren't unkind, exactly. But there’s a distance. It’s like they have a script they have to follow, and if I start to ask questions that stray too far from their flow, they get a bit impatient. I remember asking about delayed cord clamping or wanting to move around during labor, and I saw that look—that "don't-you-worry-about-the-details, we’ve got it handled" look. It makes me feel like my autonomy is an inconvenience to their schedule.

It makes me miss the idea of a midwife who would just sit with you, who knew your history and your fears. Instead, I see whoever is on rotation. It’s a gamble every single time. Sometimes I get someone who looks me in the eye, and for ten minutes, I feel like I’m a person again. Other times, I’m just a chart. It’s hard to build trust in a system that seems to view the "patient" as the last person who should have a say in the process.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

It’s rarely the same person. It’s like a carousel of faces—a revolving door of medical professionals who see me for maybe fifteen minutes, tops. One day it’s a nurse practitioner I’ve never met, the next it’s an OB who looks like they’re rushing to get to a surgery. 

It feels incredibly isolating. Every time I walk in, I have to re-explain myself. I have to re-state my anxieties, my history, and my hopes, as if I’m reading from a script to someone who hasn't bothered to skim my file. There’s no continuity, no sense that anyone is actually *invested* in the outcome beyond making sure the vitals are within the "normal" range. 

How does it feel? It feels like being an anonymous cog in a machine. I’m at the mercy of whoever’s name happens to be on the schedule that day. How can I possibly trust a system to catch me when I’m vulnerable during labor if they can’t even remember my name when I’m fully clothed and sitting in a chair? I feel like I’m constantly shouting into a void, trying to convince them that I’m a human with a life and a history, not just a set of physical symptoms waiting to be managed. It’s deeply lonely.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

The screening feels like a gauntlet. Every time they offer a test—the genetic screenings, the glucose tolerance tests, the constant scans—it’s presented as a mandatory step on a checklist rather than a conversation. It’s phrased like, "We need to check this," not "Would you like to know this?"

Making sense of it is… hard. I’m not a doctor, and I don’t have a partner to help me dissect the pros and cons. I go home and I find myself deep in internet rabbit holes at 2:00 AM, feeling that familiar knot of anxiety. I’m terrified of missing something important, of being the mother who didn't do "enough" for her child. But at the same time, I’m deeply suspicious of the "over-medicalization" of it all. Is this test actually for me, or is it for the hospital’s liability?

I’ve had to be my own advocate, which is exhausting when you’re already tired from working and just trying to survive. I usually end up saying yes because the pressure to be a "compliant patient" is so immense. If I refuse, I’m seen as "difficult" or "uninformed." So, I let them do the tests, but I do it with this internal resentment, feeling like I’m handing over more of my agency just to keep the machinery of the hospital running smoothly. It’s a bitter trade-off.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

I mostly turn to the quiet corners of the internet—the forums where other women share their *real* experiences, not the sterilized, sugar-coated brochures the clinic hands out. When I’m reading the hospital’s pamphlets, I feel like I’m reading a manual for an appliance, not a guide for my own body. The language is so detached, so clinical. It tells me *what* will happen, but never *why* or how I might actually feel about it.

I spend a lot of time reading about the history of midwifery. Knowing that there was a time when women were the experts of their own births—where they sat in circles and held space for one another—that matters more to me than any automated advice. It helps me remember that I’m part of a lineage, not just a modern anomaly. 

Support is... well, it’s mostly just me. I don’t have a partner to debrief with after an appointment, so I carry all these questions and fears around in my head until they start to weigh me down. I’ve started writing things down in a notebook, trying to keep track of my own thoughts so I don't lose myself in the noise of the clinic. That notebook is the only place where I’m allowed to be Elna, without a "G2" label attached. It’s my only real source of truth right now.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

I use a couple of online forums, though I try to be careful. There’s a community for mothers who are trying to navigate the "system" while feeling like outsiders, and that’s been a lifeline. When I get a cold, dismissive response at the clinic, I go home and search for similar experiences. Finding someone else who felt "processed" instead of "cared for" makes me feel a little less crazy. It validates that my frustration isn't just me being "difficult"—it's a shared reality.

What I get there that I can’t get from the doctors is the *truth* about the emotional labor of birth. The doctors and the brochures only talk about biological benchmarks—cervical dilation, heart rates, blood pressure. They never talk about the terror of feeling like you’re losing control, or the way you’re expected to just "be a patient" and stop asking questions. In those forums, women talk about their fears, their anger at being ignored, and how they reclaimed their voice. 

It’s not just medical advice; it’s permission to care about my own autonomy. It reminds me that the white-walled, cold efficiency of the clinic isn't the only way things can be. It’s helped me formulate the questions I *want* to ask, even if I'm still terrified to actually say them out loud to my provider.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It’s a strange, heavy duality. Physically, I feel like a stranger in my own skin. Every week there’s a new ache—my ribs, my back, the way my breath hitches when I walk up the stairs. It’s a constant, grounding reminder that I’m growing a human, but it also makes me feel vulnerable. When you’re low-income and working shifts, being "physically compromised" feels like a liability. I feel like my body is betraying my ability to just keep my head above water.

Emotionally, it’s even more complicated. There’s this awe, of course—I touch my belly and I know there’s life in there—but that’s often eclipsed by this protective, fierce resentment toward the medical establishment. I feel like my body is being audited. Every time they pull out that tape measure or strap those monitors on, I feel like they’re trying to claim territory. 

I’ve had to work really hard to make sure I’m not just "a pregnant body" in their eyes. I try to talk to the baby, to sing, to move in ways that feel like *me* and not like a patient performing for a doctor. I’m relating to these changes by trying to treat my body as my own sanctuary, even while I’m forced to hand it over to strangers in scrubs. It’s a battle of ownership.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

The exhaustion is the hardest part to reconcile. It’s not just the "tiredness" everyone talks about; it’s this bone-deep weariness that comes from the weight of everything—the physical burden of the baby, the financial stress, and the constant, draining vigilance required to navigate the clinic. 

When you’re pregnant and poor, your body feels like an expensive machine you can’t afford to maintain. I find myself feeling guilty for needing rest because I’m so worried about my job, about the rent, about keeping my life together. It makes me feel like I’m failing. I look in the mirror and I see my changing shape—the belly, the swelling—and instead of feeling beautiful or "blessed," I feel like a liability. 

It’s hard to reconcile the joy of wanting this baby with the physical reality of being pushed to my absolute limits every single day. I hate that my body is so visible, so exposed, and so scrutinized by strangers, when at the same time, it’s the only place where I’m truly safe. It feels like an invasion. I wanted this pregnancy to feel like a strength, but some days, it just feels like the thing that’s finally going to break me.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

I’m on my own for this one. Being unmarried and having to navigate this... let's just say, the father isn't in the picture. My family is supportive in their own way, I guess, but they live far and they’ve got their own struggles, so I try not to dump the heavy stuff on them. I don't want them to worry, and honestly, they wouldn't understand the specific, institutional dread I’m feeling. They’d just tell me to "trust the doctors" because that’s what you do, right? You trust the experts.

It feels very singular. When I’m sitting in the waiting room at the clinic, I’m the only one without a hand to hold. Everyone else has a partner there, someone to catch the doctor’s comments or help them put their shoes back on. I’m the woman in the corner with my notebook, trying to look steady. 

It’s isolating, but there’s a strange clarity to it, too. Because I don't have anyone else to advocate for me, I know that if I don't speak up, no one will. I’ve become my own primary support. It’s not how I imagined it, but I’ve realized that relying on myself is the only way I’m going to get through this without feeling completely erased.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

Well, since I don’t have a partner, the "support" dynamic is essentially just me staring back at a system that expects a two-parent, two-income, stable-home standard. It’s funny, actually—or maybe it’s just sad—how the clinic staff handles it. 

Sometimes, when I mention that I’m on my own, there’s this look of pity that flashes across their faces before they mask it with professional efficiency. It makes me feel like a "problem" they need to solve rather than a mother they need to support. They often ask, "Who will be your labor partner?" or "Who is helping you with the prep?" and every time I answer, I feel like I'm confirming their bias that my pregnancy is 'high-risk' in some way—not medically, but socially.

I think the system is built for a pair. It’s built for one person to be the "patient" and the other to be the "advocate" or the "witness." Since I have to be both, I feel like I’m always split in half. I’m trying to listen to the midwife’s instructions while simultaneously trying to remember the questions I wrote in my notebook. It’s a lot to hold. I’ve stopped expecting them to include "me" and started focusing on not letting them dismiss me. It’s a lonely way to prepare for birth.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

My biggest fear isn't actually the labor itself. It’s the fear of being silenced when I’m at my most vulnerable. I’m terrified that I’ll be in that hospital bed, in pain and exhausted, and I’ll try to say "no" to a procedure I don’t want—like an induction or an episiotomy—and they’ll just look at me like I’m a child who doesn't understand "the science." That fear of having my autonomy stripped away, like it was with my first, keeps me up at night. 

I worry about the money, obviously. What if something goes wrong and I end up with a bill that keeps me in debt for years? That’s a real, tangible threat that makes the whole process feel heavy. I’m also scared that in the rush of the hospital, they’ll see me as a "G2, low income, single mother" and decide I’m not worth the extra time to listen to. 

I’m scared that my voice will be drowned out by the machines. I want so badly to have a moment of power—to be the one who brings my baby into the world—rather than just being the passive recipient of medical care. The thought of losing that, of being reduced to a footnote in a chart, is what keeps me on edge every single day.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

I tried, once. Just a few weeks ago, I worked up the courage to mention that I felt traumatized by my first birth—that the interventions felt like they happened *to* me, rather than with me. I told them that this time, I really wanted to be heard, to have a say in how things unfold. 

The midwife just gave me this practiced, polite nod. She told me, "We’ll cross that bridge when we get to it," and "Don't worry, we’ll do what's best for you and the baby." It felt like she was patting me on the head. She wasn't malicious, but she completely dismissed the *meaning* of what I was saying. To her, "what's best" is a clinical term, defined by hospital policy and risk-aversion. To me, "what's best" involves my own peace of mind, my own agency, and my own body’s wisdom.

When I heard her say that, I just stopped. I realized that my fears are not part of their clinical framework. They aren't something to be "solved" or "treated," so they just get smoothed over with platitudes. I felt so small after that interaction. It confirmed that if I want to have a different experience this time, I can't rely on them to facilitate it. I have to be my own guardrail. It's exhausting, but I’ve stopped sharing my fears with them—it’s safer to keep them in my notebook.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

My "birth plan" feels less like a set of demands and more like a desperate, scribbled map for a path I’m trying to walk in the dark. I didn’t use one of those pretty, pre-printed templates you see on social media—those feel like they’re for women who have the money to pay for a doula or a private birth center. Instead, I’ve been researching what my rights actually are in the hospital. I’ve been reading up on delayed cord clamping, skin-to-skin contact, and the importance of not being rushed into interventions just because the staff has a shift change coming up.

I’m trying to prepare mentally by tapping into what I call my "ancestral agency." I read about how women birthed for centuries, trusting their bodies, and I try to imagine myself doing the same, even under those harsh, sterile lights. I want this birth to be mine—not something the OB *does* to me, but something I *do*. 

But honestly? Planning for it feels like preparing for a battlefield. I’m trying to anticipate where they’ll try to push me, where they’ll try to coerce me into a "standard" procedure, and how I can firmly but politely say "no." It’s hard to prepare for a moment that’s supposed to be about love when you're so busy preparing for a fight.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

The labor started in the middle of the night—a sharp, insistent tug that I recognized immediately. I was terrified of going in too early, of being that "difficult" patient who shows up before they're ready to start the clock, but eventually, the pain grew too steady to ignore. 

When I finally arrived, the hospital was exactly as I’d feared: quiet, sterile, and cold. I felt like I was checking into a hotel where the staff didn't know I was coming and didn't really care. I had my notebook in my bag, and for the first few hours, I held onto it like a lifeline. I tried to move, to sway, to find that rhythm I’d read about—but they kept insisting I stay on the bed for the monitors. "For safety," they said. *Their* safety, maybe. 

It was a blur of voices—nurses I’d never met, residents coming in and out. Every time I asked for a moment, or tried to express what I needed, I was met with that same practiced, bureaucratic calm. They were so efficient, so detached. They didn't see me; they saw a progress chart. By the end, when the baby was finally here, I felt hollowed out—not just by the birth, but by the battle I’d fought against the very people who were supposed to be holding space for me. I was relieved it was over, but the victory felt small, quiet, and deeply stolen.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

The pain was... it was a landscape I had to navigate alone. When I arrived, the conversation about "management" wasn't a conversation at all—it was a menu of medical interventions presented like I was choosing a dish at a restaurant. They offered me an epidural almost immediately, as if that was the "standard" way to solve the "problem" of labor pain. 

I told them I wanted to try without it for as long as I could, to feel the rhythm of my own body. The nurse looked at me like I was making things unnecessarily difficult for myself, and for her. She kept asking, "Are you sure? Why suffer?" 

I felt this pressure, this subtle push to just *take* the medication so things would speed up, so they could get the room cleared, so I would stop being an outlier. Every time I hit a peak in the pain, they were there with the IV, hovering, waiting for me to break. Eventually, the exhaustion from fighting the system, combined with the sheer intensity of the labor, wore me down. I ended up choosing to take what they offered just so I could get a moment of peace from their constant, hovering presence. 

It wasn't a choice made in empowerment; it was a surrender to the path of least resistance. I wanted that relief, sure, but I hated that the price of it was losing the last bit of control I had over my own sensations. It felt like I was being managed, not cared for.
Were there options you wanted that weren't available, or options you were steered towards that you weren't sure about?

The options I really wanted weren't even on the table—they were things like quiet, dim lighting, being able to walk in the hallway, or even just having someone present who wasn't a stranger watching a screen. I wanted to be in a space that felt like a sanctuary, not an operating theater. But every time I moved, or tried to change positions to find a rhythm that felt natural, I was steered back to that bed, back to the monitor, back to the "protocol." 

I felt constantly steered toward an induction, too. The language they used was all about "efficiency"—"let's get things moving," "we want to see more progress," "to be safe." It felt like they were trying to sync my body to their clock, not the other way around. I wasn't sure about any of it. I had read that spontaneous labor is often better for both the baby and the mother, but they made it sound like I was being negligent for wanting to wait. 

It’s that feeling of being a "difficult" patient again. When I hesitated, they looked at me with this mix of pity and annoyance, like I was holding up a process I didn't understand. I wanted choices that made me feel empowered, but all I was steered towards were choices that made their job easier. That’s the real sting of it—being treated like a logistical hurdle in my own birth.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

I was completely on my own. My family wasn't there, and I didn't have a partner to hold my hand or to stand between me and the staff. It was just me and the rotation of faces—doctors and nurses who cycled in and out like they were just checking the oil in a car. 

Their presence didn’t feel like "support" in any real sense; it felt like surveillance. There was no one there to say, "Elna, you're doing great," or to just sit in the silence with me. Instead, every time someone walked into that room, it was for a clinical purpose—to check a monitor, to record a number, to shift a line. I felt like an intruder in my own labor. I kept looking at the door, hoping someone would walk in who actually knew me—who knew that I was a person, not just a bed number—but that person never came.

It was profoundly lonely. I wanted a witness, someone who cared about the *experience* of my transition into motherhood, but I only had experts interested in the *mechanics* of it. I had to be my own anchor, my own advocate, and my own cheerleader, while also being the person in the bed doing all the work. It’s a weight I think I’ll carry for a long time.
Was there a particular person — whether a professional or someone close to you — whose presence made a real difference? What did they do?

There was one nurse, right toward the very end, who broke the script for a few minutes. I don’t even remember her name—maybe Sarah? She came in during a shift change, and for once, she didn't rush over to the monitors or start scrolling through my chart on the screen. She just stood there by the bed for a second, looked at me, and asked, "How are you doing, Elna? Really?"

I think I was so conditioned to be defensive that I almost didn't know what to say. But she didn't try to "fix" anything. She just placed a cool cloth on my forehead and held my hand for a moment while I was catching my breath. It wasn’t a medical intervention; it was just... humanity. She stayed for a few minutes past when she needed to, and she didn't look at the clock once.

In that small moment, the room felt different. The walls didn't feel so high, and the machines didn't feel so loud. She treated me like a mother, not a patient. It made such a difference because it was the only time I felt seen. I wasn't just "G2" to her; I was just a person having a baby. It didn't change the outcome, or the system, but it made me feel a little less like I was fighting a war on my own.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

The birth ended with a blur of fluorescent lights and the sudden, sharp cry of my baby—the only part of the whole ordeal that felt real, the only part that wasn't filtered through hospital protocol. It wasn't the birth I had envisioned in my notebook; it was a clinical extraction, efficient and sterile, stripped of the intimacy I’d hoped for. 

In the immediate aftermath, I felt a strange mixture of relief and profound, hollow grief. I had survived, and my baby was here, but I felt like I had left a part of my own agency behind on that delivery table. The staff moved on quickly, already prepping for the next "case," and I was left alone in that recovery room. I remember staring at the ceiling, feeling the silence of the hospital settle around me, and wondering why it felt so much like a defeat. 

I was happy—oh, God, I was so happy to finally have my child in my arms—but I was also angry. I felt like I had been forced to trade my autonomy for a safe delivery, like the system had "managed" me out of my own experience. I was a mother, finally, but I felt like I was waking up from a dream I hadn't been allowed to write.
If things didn't go to plan — or if there were interventions or complications — how were those explained or communicated to you at the time?

Communication in that room felt like a one-way street. When things started to drift from what I had hoped for—when they started talking about "augmentation" because I wasn't dilating fast enough to suit the shift’s schedule—it wasn't a conversation about my body or my needs. It was an announcement. 

They’d say things like, "We need to break your water to get things moving," or "It’s time for some Pitocin to help you along." The "why" was always the same: it was for the sake of the timeline, for "safety," or because the monitor showed something they didn't like. They never asked, "How does this feel to you?" or "What do you think?" They communicated in directives, not options. If I pushed back even a little, the tone would shift—they’d become more clinical, more authoritative, and suddenly I was the one who was being "unreasonable" or "risky."

It was explained as a series of necessary steps to reach the finish line, never as a journey I was participating in. They treated me like a patient who had forgotten the rules, not a mother giving birth. It’s hard to feel like you’re making informed decisions when the only information you’re given is designed to steer you toward their protocol, not your peace of mind. It felt less like medicine and more like management.
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

The room was a landscape of chrome and beige, clinical to the point of erasure. Every time I looked at the monitors, those jagged lines reminded me that I was a biological process being observed by strangers, not a woman welcoming a new life. The lighting was the worst part—those buzzing, overhead fluorescents that stripped away any softness, any privacy. They felt like an interrogation lamp that never turned off. 

I kept thinking, *this is where they make mothers?* It felt so industrial. There were no soft edges, no sense of home, no indication that this was supposed to be one of the most profound moments of my life. It was a space designed for throughput, for cleaning, for the next shift to come in and take over. I remember the smell—that sharp, sterile scent of disinfectant that just cuts through everything. 

It never felt like the right place for me. I wanted a quiet, shadowed space where I could retreat into my own body, but the hospital doesn’t leave room for "retreat." Everything is exposed. I felt like a specimen pinned to a board. I think if I’d been in a room that felt even remotely human—maybe with a dim light or just a bit of silence—it wouldn't have felt like such a battle. It was a place designed to keep me compliant, and unfortunately, it worked.
How much choice did you have about where you gave birth — and did you have access to the options you wanted?

I didn’t really have a "choice" at all—not in any real, meaningful sense. When you’re living on a tight income and relying on the insurance that covers only the most basic, standard-issue care, your options are basically non-existent. There was no touring private birth centers with soft lighting and home-like furniture; there was only the hospital the insurance dictated, the one that felt like a factory.

I dreamed of a place where I could move freely, maybe even somewhere with a tub or just a sense of privacy, but that’s not what’s covered. Those are "boutique" amenities. The system tells you that for "your safety," you have to be in a place where they have the machines and the surgical backup, but that safety feels like a cage. 

Access? I had no access to the kind of environment that would have allowed me to feel like a person. Every time I looked at those high, sterile walls, I knew exactly what I was: a patient in a system that doesn't account for anything but the bottom line. It wasn't about what I wanted or what would have helped me feel brave; it was about what the institution provided. You take what you can get, and you swallow the rest.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

When I first let myself really think about being pregnant, I imagined a partnership. I wanted a midwife who would sit across from me—not behind a computer screen—and actually ask me about my fears and my dreams for the birth. I wanted a relationship built on trust, where I could be the expert on my own body, and they would be the expert on the medical side, and we’d meet somewhere in the middle. I wanted to feel *heard*. I wanted to feel like I wasn't just a chart number.

But deep down, I was terrified—and I think I knew, even then, that it wouldn't happen. I had this nagging, anxious sense that I wouldn't be "important" enough to them. I worried that my lack of a partner, my financial struggles, and the fact that I’m just one of a hundred women in the rotation would make me invisible. I was scared that when I walked in, they’d look at my paperwork and see "single, low-income," and automatically decide I was someone to be managed, not someone to be listened to. 

It’s painful to realize your intuition was right. I wanted a collaborator, but I ended up with a technician. I guess I hoped the medical system had evolved to see the person behind the symptoms, but it turns out the system is just as cold and rigid as it’s always been.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

Those conversations… they were pretty much just me, sitting alone in my apartment, staring at the walls and doing math. There wasn't really a "we" to talk to. I didn't have a partner to dream with or to plan a timeline with. It was just me looking at my bank account, my work schedule, and the terrifying, empty space where a support system should be.

I felt like I had all the say, but none of the control. It wasn't like a deliberate, romantic choice to "start a family"—it was this deep, primal ache to be a mother, battling against the cold, hard reality of my life. I think that’s why I felt so much pressure to be "perfect" once I got pregnant. I felt like if I didn't do everything exactly right—if I didn't handle the appointments, the job, the money, and the birth perfectly—then the system would have every reason to look down on me, or worse, to take my choices away entirely.

It never felt "right," but it felt inevitable. I wanted this baby more than I feared the system, and that's the only reason I went through with it. I just wish I’d had someone in my corner to help me carry the weight of those decisions instead of having to be the only one making them.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

My mother... she’s the one who really drilled the "don't rock the boat" mentality into me. She grew up in an era where the doctor was practically God. If he told you to lie down, you laid down. If he told you to take this or that, you didn't ask questions. She always talked about birth like it was this dangerous, mysterious, medical event that you’re lucky to survive, and that the hospital is the only place where you're "safe."

Even though I knew—or at least, I *felt*—that there was a better way, her voice was always in the back of my mind. Whenever I considered pushing back against a doctor's suggestion, I’d hear her saying, "Elna, don't be difficult. They’re the experts, they know what they’re doing." It made me feel like if I demanded more—if I asked for a birth plan that focused on my autonomy—I was being ungrateful, or reckless with my baby’s life.

It’s a heavy legacy to carry. I wanted to be a modern mother, to be empowered and informed, but I was constantly battling that deep-seated, generational fear that if you don't do exactly what the institution tells you, you’re failing at the most important task of your life. It’s hard to trust your own instincts when you’ve been raised to believe that the system is the only thing standing between you and disaster.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before I was pregnant, I honestly felt like my body was just... mine. It was the thing that carried me to work, the thing that got me through my days. I didn't think about it in terms of "performance" or "medical viability." I just existed in it.

When I started thinking about pregnancy, that shifted into something sharper and more complicated. I began to view my body as this vessel that had to be "ready" or "capable" in a way that satisfied a checklist I didn't even write. I worried if it was healthy enough, strong enough, or "normal" enough to get through the system without intervention. Suddenly, my body wasn't just *me* anymore; it became a project to be managed.

Once I was actually pregnant, that feeling turned into a tug-of-war. I’d have these moments of absolute, primal power—feeling the baby move, knowing I was doing the work of creating life—but the clinic would immediately reframe it as a medical process. They made my body feel like a machine that might break down at any second. I had to fight to keep my own sense of capability alive. I wanted to trust that my body knew what to do, but I was constantly being told—by the charts, the tests, the indifferent faces—that only *they* knew how to make it work. It was an assault on my own agency.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

Looking back, those routine visits were like little warning shots I didn't want to hear. I remember going in for a simple check-up, just a standard thing, and feeling that same detached, conveyor-belt speed. The provider would walk in, barely look at me, tap at their computer, and ask the same five questions. I’d try to mention something else, maybe something small, and the look they’d give me—that "you’re taking up too much of my time" look—really stuck.

It gave me this sinking feeling that maternity care would be even worse. If they couldn't be bothered to really listen to me when I was just a routine patient, how could I expect them to treat me like a person when I was in the middle of the most vulnerable, high-stakes moment of my life? I had this premonition that once the "pregnant" label was slapped on my chart, I’d be even further removed from being a human in their eyes. 

I was right, of course. It’s the same institutional coldness, just dialed up to eleven because now there’s a baby to "manage," too. It’s hard to feel like you’re entering a journey of motherhood when you’re actually just entering a system that treats you like a liability from day one.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

I didn’t really have many conversations about it, if I’m honest. It felt like something I had to keep close to my chest because if I talked about it too much, it would stop being a dream and start being another "problem" to explain to people who wouldn't understand my reality. 

My friends were mostly just trying to keep their own heads above water, working shifts and paying rent, so we’d joke about how "impossible" it seemed to even think about kids when you’re barely making it on your own. It felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, which made the idea of it feel both sacred and illicit. 

I think the only person whose input really mattered—in a way—was my own intuition. I didn't have a partner to balance things out, and my mother’s voice was always there, full of that old-fashioned, fearful obedience to the medical system. I had to learn to tune her out. That was the most meaningful part of the process, actually: realizing that the loudest voices in my life—the clinic, my family—were the ones that made me feel the smallest. I had to build a quiet space inside myself where I could believe that my body was capable, even if everyone around me treated it like it was broken. It was a lonely way to start, but I think it was the only way I could keep a sliver of myself for the baby.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

You know, before I was ever actually pregnant, I was always hyper-aware of how my body didn't "fit" the mold. I grew up in a world where doctors and nurses—even when I was just going in for a cold or a check-up—always made me feel like I was a bit of a statistical outlier. Because I didn't have a stable life, or money, or a partner, I think I absorbed the idea that my body was inherently "at risk" or "problematic." 

I had this weird, persistent feeling that if I ever did get pregnant, my body wouldn’t be allowed to just *do its thing*. I felt like I was physically carrying the weight of being "low-income"—it’s not just a status, it’s a way people look at you. I worried that my health history, which was honestly fine, would be scrutinized and picked apart just because of who I was. 

I think the uncertainty came from knowing that the system doesn't trust women like me. I felt like I had to be "extra" healthy, "extra" compliant, and "extra" careful just to be treated with a baseline level of respect. It made the whole idea of pregnancy feel less like a natural transition and more like an audition for a role I wasn't sure I was allowed to play.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

Honestly, no. There wasn't a single person—not a doctor, not a friend, not a mentor—who felt like a "home base" for my questions. My life has been such a patchwork of temporary jobs and moving apartments that I’ve never really had that kind of stability. 

When I’d have a question or a worry—even something as simple as, "Is this weird thing with my period normal?"—I was always my own first and last stop. I’d look it up, read ten different forums, and try to piece together an answer that felt right. The doctors were always so rushed and so focused on the next patient that I learned early on not to bother them with "what if" questions. It felt like asking for their time was an imposition.

It’s really lonely, realizing that you’ve been navigating your health by yourself for so long. You’d think that with something as major as pregnancy, the system would provide someone, but it’s just more of the same. I kept looking for a guide, someone who would say, "I’m here for you, ask me anything," but that person doesn't exist in the world I live in. I’ve had to become my own reliable source, which is just another way of saying I’m the only one who cares about the answers.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

I’m sorry, I think there might be a misunderstanding. I said there *wasn't* anyone—not a professional or a friend—who filled that role for me. I’ve been entirely on my own, and honestly, that’s exactly the problem.

If I *had* found someone who felt reliable, it would have been because they didn't treat me like a file folder or a set of symptoms. Trust isn't built by a doctor giving you a list of "do's and don'ts" or by a brochure telling you what to expect. Trust is built in the spaces where they *don’t* rush you. It’s built when someone actually pauses, looks you in the eye, and validates that your fear is real, rather than just brushing it off with "we’ll cross that bridge later."

I’ve spent my life looking for that—for someone who treats my questions like they actually matter. But instead, every interaction I’ve had, especially with the healthcare system, has been transactional. They give me a service, I give them my compliance, and we both try to get it over with as quickly as possible. That isn't trust; that’s just a trade. And it’s a trade where I’m always the one who loses something.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

That’s a heavy question. People like to talk about "choice" as if it’s this pure, blank slate, but for me, it felt more like trying to paint in the middle of a hurricane. Was I free? In a way, sure—nobody forced me. But was it "in my own time and my own way"? Absolutely not.

When you’re constantly juggling the rent, shift work, and the feeling that your life is something that’s happening *to* you, the "right time" never really arrives. There’s always another year of working, another promotion I don’t get, another way to try and build a buffer that never quite builds. If I had waited until I felt "ready"—until I had the perfect savings or a partner or a house—I’d likely be waiting forever.

There was this quiet, constant pressure from the clock, too. You see your body changing, you see your friends moving into different stages of their lives, and you feel the weight of your own circumstances pressing in. I felt like I had to grab at this, even if the timing was messy and the foundation was shaky, because if I waited for the "ideal" circumstances, I might lose the chance altogether. 

It was a choice, yes, but it was a choice made from a place of limited options. It was mine, but it was forged under the pressure of a system that wasn’t built to make it easy for someone like me.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

The timing never feels like it's in your hands when you're living paycheck to paycheck. Every month is a calculation of whether I can afford to exist, let alone bring another life into this world. I kept waiting for that mythical moment where my bank balance would stop being a source of panic, but in this job market, that moment is a moving target. 

Then there's the relationship side of it. Being single in a world that assumes everyone is part of a tidy, two-parent unit is a constant, nagging friction. I didn't want to wait for a partner to "arrive" because there were no guarantees he ever would, but that reality hit hard—knowing that if I did this, I’d be doing it entirely solo, with zero backup if I got sick, or tired, or needed a hand. 

Culturally, there's this unspoken pressure that if you aren't "settled"—with the ring, the house, the stability—then you’re somehow behind. I felt like I was in a race I’d already lost. It wasn't that I didn't want the baby; it’s that I felt like I was being judged for the *circumstances* of my life as much as for the pregnancy itself. The system treats you like you’re making a mistake if you don’t have all your ducks in a row. It makes you feel small, and it makes every decision feel like a gamble.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I felt like I was in the passenger seat, blindfolded, while someone else was driving, and they were constantly ignoring my directions. I went in wanting to be the one in control—my notebook in my bag, my questions prepared—but the moment I walked through those clinic doors, the power dynamic shifted.

I handed over the wheel, but it was rarely willing. When they told me, "We need to do this scan," or "You need this blood panel," it was framed as a necessity, not an invitation. If I tried to hesitate, the implicit threat was always there: *if you don’t do this, you’re being negligent.* I’d feel myself stiffen, my voice getting smaller, and I’d just sign the form or roll up my sleeve because the exhaustion of fighting them was heavier than the feeling of losing my autonomy. 

There were tiny, quiet moments where I tried to reclaim it—refusing a certain supplement or asking to be heard on a minor point—but those felt like flickers, not actual control. The system is designed to make you feel like your participation is a formality, not a choice. I spent the entire pregnancy oscillating between wanting to be a fierce advocate for my own body and just wanting to survive the process without them judging me or marking me as "non-compliant." It’s an impossible, lonely way to grow a life.
Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?

It’s a strange, bitter trade-off. There were times when the exhaustion was so thick I felt like I was drowning—when the financial math, the work shifts, and the physical reality of the pregnancy all piled up—and in those moments, just letting someone else take the lead felt like a brief, shaky breath of air. When they’d just tell me, "Everything is fine, we’ve got this," I wanted to believe them so badly that I let myself drift. It was a relief to not have to hold the weight of every single decision, every risk, and every fear for just a few minutes.

But that relief always soured into a cold, hollow feeling afterward. That’s when I realized I’d traded my voice for a moment of ease. It felt like I was handing them the pen to my own story, and they were writing in a language I didn’t recognize. Every time I relaxed my grip, the system pushed me further into the "standard" box, stripping away the little things that made the experience feel like mine. 

It was like being on a raft in rough water; letting the current take me was less work, but it was steering me toward a shore I didn’t want to land on. I ended up feeling like a spectator in my own pregnancy, and that’s a hard thing to forgive yourself for later.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

I think you might have forgotten—there isn't a partner in the picture. It was just me, all the way through. That’s the core of the loneliness, really. There wasn't anyone to be "on the same page" with, or to help me weigh the pros and cons. 

When a decision came up—like whether or not to do a certain screening—the "we" was just the echo of my own brain talking to itself at two in the morning. I’d be in bed, my stomach tight, trying to figure out if I was making a choice for the baby or if I was just trying to avoid being labeled "difficult" by the clinic staff. If I’d had a partner, maybe the burden of that choice wouldn't have felt so heavy, or maybe I’d have had someone to help me stand my ground when the staff tried to push me toward their preferred "standard" path.

Instead, I felt like I was constantly split. One part of me wanted to be the fierce, informed advocate, and the other part just wanted the appointment to be over so I could go home and stop feeling like I was under a microscope. It’s a very solitary kind of exhausting. I didn't have the luxury of navigating this with anyone else; I was the captain, the navigator, and the one getting hit by the waves, all at the same time.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

I know you’re trying to understand the dynamic, but I need to be clear: there was no "we" to have these discussions with. I was completely, utterly on my own. 

When you ask which decisions "mattered" to a partner, it just highlights how the whole system is built for a couple. Every single form asked for a "partner's information," every class I looked into assumed I’d have someone sitting next to me, and every time I walked into an exam room, the doctor would look at the empty chair beside me with that silent, pitying question. 

If I *had* had someone there, the decisions about risk—like the genetic testing or the induction—would have been the ones I’d want shared. Those are the heavy, terrifying choices where the weight of "what if" is just too much to carry alone. But the ones that were *my* call? The ones that really mattered to my soul? Those would have been the ones about my body—how I wanted to move during labor, the lights, the privacy, the music. Those felt like an extension of my identity, and even if I’d had a partner, I think I would have fought to keep those purely mine. 

It’s just… it’s a different kind of motherhood when there’s no one to lean on. You don’t get to delegate the fear. You hold it all. Every time.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the thing that sticks with me most—the thing I keep coming back to—is how much I felt like I had to "perform" during this whole process. I wasn’t just a woman growing a life; I was a student taking a test I never asked for, in a room where the rules were constantly changing, and I didn't have the cheat sheet. 

People always talk about "the miracle of birth," but they don't talk about the sheer, grueling *labor* of trying to keep your dignity intact while a system—a hospital, a clinical protocol, an insurance algorithm—is trying to strip it away, one appointment at a time. It felt like I was fighting for my right to be a mother on my own terms, not just a "case" to be cleared.

If I could leave you with one thing, it’s this: the care isn't just in the medicine. It’s in the quiet, in the listening, in the acknowledgement that the person in the bed has a life and a story that’s far bigger than the G2 chart in their hand. We aren't just specimens. We’re the authors of our own lives, and we deserve to be treated as such, even when we’re tired, and even when we’re alone. Thank you for listening. It’s rare that I get to say any of this out loud.
